branch_key: early-parent-alone

try_this_today: "Call your parent tonight — not to quiz them or check their memory, just to hear their voice. Then write down the name and number of one person who lives near them."
---

## What this is

Your parent has been diagnosed with dementia. And they live alone.

Maybe they have lived alone for years — since the divorce, since your other parent died, since they chose independence over anything else. Maybe the aloneness is new, or maybe it is the one thing they are fiercest about keeping. Either way, the diagnosis landed in a house where no one else sleeps, and you are somewhere else.

<!-- provenance: composite -->
Adult children in this situation often describe the first few days as a running calculation they cannot turn off. Is the stove on? Did they take their medication? Are they eating? Did they lock the door? The questions loop, especially at night, and there is no way to answer them from where you are. You may find yourself checking your phone constantly, or calling and then feeling worse when they sound fine — because "fine" no longer means what it used to mean. The gap between what you can see and what you can know is where the anxiety lives.

How the diagnosis was reached may look different from what you expected. The workup usually includes a history, a cognitive screen (often the MoCA, MMSE, or Mini-Cog), standard bloodwork, and imaging. In 2025–2026, many clinicians also use a blood biomarker test — measuring phosphorylated tau — as part of the workup [1][2][3]. As of 2025, two are FDA-cleared: the Fujirebio Lumipulse pTau217/Aβ42 ratio [2] and the Roche Elecsys pTau181 [3]. The Alzheimer's Association published its first clinical guideline on these blood tests in July 2025, reporting agreement with PET brain scans 91.7% of the time when positive and 97.3% when negative [1]. Your parent's doctor may have used a blood test, a PET scan, a spinal tap, or a combination. The method does not change what to do this week.

## Why it matters now

When your parent lives alone, the first two weeks carry a different weight than they do for someone in the same house. You are not managing daily friction — you are managing distance and uncertainty. The biggest risk is not that you will do too much too fast. It is that you will either rush to move them (selling their house, uprooting their life in the first week) or freeze and do nothing because the problem feels too big to touch from where you sit.

The 2024 Lancet Commission found that timely support after diagnosis reduces long-term caregiver distress [7]. For families managing from a distance, "timely" means this week is for gathering information — not making permanent changes. Your parent's living situation does not have to change today. But you do need to know, concretely, what their daily setup looks like right now.

<!-- provenance: composite -->
Many caregivers managing remotely describe a specific guilt: the feeling that if they really cared enough, they would move closer, or move the parent in, or quit their job and be there. That guilt is real, and it is not useful. The research does not say that co-living produces better outcomes across the board. It says that informed oversight, reliable check-ins, and a local safety net matter. Those things can be built from a distance. Not easily, but they can be built.

The things that genuinely can wait — restructuring finances, choosing a facility, having the big sibling conversation, changing the will — are listed in the **Things That Can Wait** checklist. Nothing on that list gets worse if you take ten days before acting.

## What to do this week

Your action items look different from someone who lives with their parent. You are not setting boundaries in shared space — you are mapping the space from outside. Pick one or two of these. The **First Two Weeks Action Items** checklist has the full details.

**Write down what the clinician said.** If you were at the appointment, do this now: the type of dementia named (or "not yet specified"), any medications started, tests pending, and follow-up date (usually 2–4 weeks) [5]. If you were not at the appointment, call the clinic. You are allowed to ask. If your parent has signed a HIPAA release naming you, the clinic can speak with you directly. If they have not, getting that release is one of the first concrete tasks — ask your parent to call the clinic with you on the line, or bring a signed authorization to the next visit.

**Figure out the medication situation.** This is urgent in a way it would not be if someone else were in the house. How does your parent manage medications right now? A pill organizer? Memory? Nothing? If a new medication was started at the diagnostic visit, you need to know: can they take it reliably on their own? A weekly pill organizer filled by a visiting family member or neighbor, or a pharmacy that offers blister packs, can bridge this for now [5][6].

**Identify one local person.** Not a caregiver — a contact. A neighbor who sees your parent regularly. A friend from church. A building manager. Someone who is physically near and who would notice if the mail piled up or the lights stayed off. Call that person. You do not have to share the diagnosis — you can simply say, "My parent has a health situation, and I would feel better knowing someone nearby is keeping an eye out. Can I give you my number?" [6]. If no such person exists, that is your most important finding this week, and it changes the planning that follows.

<!-- provenance: composite -->
Caregivers who manage remotely often discover that their parent's world has gotten smaller than they realized. The neighbor they assumed was checking in moved two years ago. The friend from the senior center stopped calling. The daily routine that sounded active on the phone turns out to be: TV, one meal, bed. This can be painful to uncover. But it is better to know it now, in the first two weeks, than to discover it during a crisis at month three.

**Do a basic safety check — in person if possible, by phone if not.** Walk through (literally or verbally) the daily hazards: Is the stove gas or electric? Is there a working smoke detector? Are there rugs they could trip on? Do they lock the door at night? Are they driving, and if so, is that still safe? [5] You do not have to fix everything you find. You are inventorying. Write it down.

**Ask the clinic if they participate in the GUIDE program.** The CMS GUIDE Model (Guiding an Improved Dementia Experience) went live at 390 organizations in July 2025 [4]. If your parent's clinic participates, you may be eligible for care navigation, caregiver education, and respite support — reimbursed up to roughly $2,500 per year (as of 2025) [4]. For distance caregivers, the care navigation component is especially valuable — it puts a professional near your parent who can help coordinate what you cannot. Check at cms.gov/guide or call the clinic.

**Locate key documents — or find out if they exist.** Driver's license or state ID, health insurance cards (including Medicare or Medicaid cards), power of attorney, healthcare proxy / advance directive. If you are not in the house, ask your parent where these are. If they do not know, or if the documents do not exist, note that for month two [5].

**Know that treatment options may come up.** Your clinician may mention disease-modifying therapies that did not exist a few years ago [7]. That is a month-one conversation — not a week-one decision.

## When to call sooner

When your parent lives alone, the threshold for calling the clinician is lower — because you do not have eyes on the situation daily. Trust your instincts. If something sounds different on the phone, that counts.

- **Sudden increased confusion over hours or days.** If your parent sounds significantly more confused than they did yesterday — disoriented about time, place, or who you are — it may be delirium. Signs include fever, sudden confusion markedly worse than baseline, or reduced alertness; a common trigger is a urinary tract infection [7]. Delirium is a medical emergency, not the disease progressing overnight, and it is treatable when caught early. If you cannot assess the situation by phone, ask your local contact to check in person, or call for a welfare check.
- **New medication side effects.** If a medication was started and your parent mentions feeling dizzy, nauseous, or "off" — or if they stop mentioning the medication at all (which may mean they stopped taking it) — call the clinic [5][6].
- **Safety concerns you learn about.** A neighbor mentions the stove was left on. Your parent mentions getting lost driving home. The mail is piling up. These are not things to file away — call the clinic. They have protocols for remote safety concerns [5].
- **Talk of self-harm or harm to others.** Call the clinic, or contact emergency services in your parent's area. That is not an overreaction [5][6].

If **you** are the one not okay — if the distance and the worry are becoming more than you can carry, if you are having thoughts of hurting yourself — contact emergency services or call/text 988 (Suicide & Crisis Lifeline). You cannot manage your parent's safety if you are not safe yourself.

## How to care for yourself in this season

The specific grief of distance caregiving is the grief of not knowing. You hang up the phone and you do not know if they ate dinner. You check the weather in their city and wonder if they remembered a coat. You lie awake running scenarios — the fall, the stove, the wandering — and none of them have endings, because you are not there to see the endings.

<!-- provenance: composite -->
What rarely gets said is that remote caregivers grieve twice: they grieve the diagnosis, and they grieve the fact that they are grieving it from somewhere else. There is a particular shame in not being present, even when the reasons are sound — your job, your family, your own health, the simple fact that your parent's house is four hundred miles away. Other people's questions make it worse: "Can't you move closer?" "Why don't they come live with you?" Those questions assume a freedom that most people do not have. The guilt they produce is corrosive, and it does not help your parent.

The Lancet Commission found that even minimal early intervention — a single counseling session, a peer-support contact, a structured information packet — reduced longer-term caregiver distress [7]. You do not need to join a support group this week. But the Alzheimer's Association (1-800-272-3900) [5] and the National Institute on Aging's ADEAR Center (1-800-438-4380) [6] both have free resources designed for caregivers at a distance, and the Association's Helpline is staffed by licensed clinicians who understand remote caregiving [5].

What helps in the first two weeks, from a distance, is not heroic action. It is reducing the unknown. Every concrete fact you learn — the name of a neighbor, the state of the stove, the location of the insurance card — takes one item off the spinning list in your head. You will not get the list to zero. But you can get it from "I have no idea what is happening" to "I know the basic shape of the situation," and that shift is what lets you sleep, which is what lets you think clearly, which is what your parent actually needs from you right now.

Do not make any permanent decisions — moving your parent, moving yourself, quitting your job (check whether you qualify for FMLA leave first [8]) — that you would not make on a regular Tuesday. The distance is real, and it will require a plan. But the plan does not have to be finished this week. The next module is there when you are ready.
